core_meaning: <ruby>事<rt>こと</rt></ruby> nominalizes verbs and adjectives, turning them into abstract 'things,' 'facts,' or 'matters.' Unlike <ruby>物<rt>もの</rt></ruby>, which refers to physical objects, <ruby>事<rt>こと</rt></ruby> refers to intangible things — situations, actions as concepts, experiences, and facts.

sentences.jp: <ruby>毎日<rt>まいにち</rt></ruby><ruby>運動<rt>うんどう</rt></ruby>することが<ruby>大切<rt>たいせつ</rt></ruby>だよ。
sentences.translation: Exercising every day is important, you know.
sentences.notes: Here <ruby>事<rt>こと</rt></ruby> nominalizes the verb <ruby>運動<rt>うんどう</rt></ruby>する, making 'exercising' a concept that can sit as the grammatical subject.

sentences.jp: あの<ruby>映画<rt>えいが</rt></ruby>を<ruby>見<rt>み</rt></ruby>たことがある？
sentences.translation: Have you ever seen that movie?
sentences.notes: 〜たことがある is one of the most common grammatical patterns using <ruby>事<rt>こと</rt></ruby>, expressing a past experience as an abstract 'fact.'

sentences.jp: <ruby>彼女<rt>かのじょ</rt></ruby>のことが<ruby>好<rt>す</rt></ruby>きです。
sentences.translation: I like her (as a person / in general).
sentences.notes: 〜のこと refers to someone or something in a holistic, abstract sense — liking 'everything about her,' not just a specific physical thing.

sentences.jp: <ruby>大事<rt>だいじ</rt></ruby>なことをメモしておいてください。
sentences.translation: Please make a note of the important things.
sentences.notes: <ruby>事<rt>こと</rt></ruby> here stands alone as a noun meaning 'matters' or 'things (intangible),' a common use in instructions and business contexts.

sentences.jp: <ruby>彼<rt>かれ</rt></ruby>が<ruby>嘘<rt>うそ</rt></ruby>をついていたことが<ruby>後<rt>あと</rt></ruby>でわかった。
sentences.translation: It turned out later that he had been lying.
sentences.notes: 〜ことがわかった is a key written/spoken pattern where <ruby>事<rt>こと</rt></ruby> packages an entire clause into a 'fact' that can be discovered or understood.

dont_use: <ruby>事<rt>こと</rt></ruby> should not replace <ruby>物<rt>もの</rt></ruby> when referring to actual physical objects — saying '<ruby>食<rt>た</rt></ruby>べることが<ruby>好<rt>す</rt></ruby>き' (I like eating) is correct, but you cannot say テーブルのことを<ruby>買<rt>か</rt></ruby>った to mean 'I bought the table.' A common learner mistake is also overusing <ruby>事<rt>こと</rt></ruby> where の works better for nominalization in some embedded clauses (e.g. <ruby>彼<rt>かれ</rt></ruby>が<ruby>走<rt>はし</rt></ruby>っているのが<ruby>見<rt>み</rt></ruby>えた, not ことが, because it describes a directly observed scene).

confused_with.contrast: <ruby>物<rt>もの</rt></ruby> refers to tangible, physical things you can touch or concrete entities, while <ruby>事<rt>こと</rt></ruby> refers to abstract things, facts, actions, and situations. Compare: <ruby>食<rt>た</rt></ruby>べ<ruby>物<rt>もの</rt></ruby> (food — a physical thing) vs. <ruby>食<rt>た</rt></ruby>べること (the act/concept of eating). When nominalizing a verb to express a general concept or experience, use <ruby>事<rt>こと</rt></ruby>; when pointing to a resulting object or something concrete, use <ruby>物<rt>もの</rt></ruby>.

frequency: <ruby>事<rt>こと</rt></ruby> is extremely common across all registers — casual speech, standard conversation, formal writing, and grammar patterns alike. It appears constantly in set expressions like 〜たことがある, 〜ことができる, 〜ことにする, and 〜ことがわかる, making it one of the most functionally important words at the N4–N3 level.
anki_hint: <ruby>事<rt>こと</rt></ruby> = an abstract 'thing' or 'fact' — if you can't hold it in your hand, it's probably <ruby>事<rt>こと</rt></ruby>, not <ruby>物<rt>もの</rt></ruby>.
